item.title: Impetus for Using GRDB
item.description:
Core Data is painful

When I first semi-earnestly started trying to program, Core Data was Apple’s answer for persisting data between app usages. I never did anything fancy with it, but it did get the job done for me once upon a time.

The problems I had with it were that a lot of it was in the XCode gui and that it had some technical baggage.

Doing anything not in text while trying to make a program, or when doing something programmatically, is a real pain. I guess it’s supposed to be beginner user friendly, and maybe avoid errors with typos, especially when you define things with strings, but it has huge downsides.

item.title: Get and Set on computed variables in Swift
item.description:
AI steers me right, and steers me wrong

I wrote a nifty article about didSet and willSet. They’re observers you can put on a property that execute a block of code whenever the property they’re attached to changes. While jumping down that well, my AI sidekick was the one that gave me the classification of these keywords which is observers. Personally, I love to know what a thing in programming is called. I sometimes find myself trying to describe a group of programming things and I don’t have the word for them. didSet and willSet aren’t really functions but I need to mention them in tandem, so having the term observer is quite useful.

item.title: didSet, and eventually willSet
item.description:
didSet

It probably wasn’t the first time I saw this function, but in the GRBD demo app, I saw this pice of code.

    /// The player ordering
    var ordering = Ordering.byScore {
        didSet { observePlayers() }
    }

It seems that didSet just fires off that closure the observePlayers function is in, but what is it doing more than just having that function within the Ordering.byScore closure?

My first attempt

Initially I tried the following

item.title: My first github repo
item.description:
I added something

I recently released my first github repo which is two functions that wrap around a some keychain functions so that someone can store a string on a users iOS (should be any Apple device) and save that information between user installations. My impetus being, I wanted to make it so I can have someone use my app once, and then have to pay to get more uses. I didn’t want them to be able to get the free use, and then uninstall and reinstall and get another free use.
